column2: {{c1::POST}} stands for Power-On Self-Test, the hardware check performed before the operating system boots.
column3: POST failures may appear as beeps or codes, but the exact meaning depends on the system firmware or manufacturer.
column4:
Why It Matters

POST is the boundary between pre-boot hardware or firmware investigation and operating-system troubleshooting.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Cloze POST Memory Source::Messer-v170

column2: A Windows Stop Error is commonly called a {{c1::Blue Screen of Death}} or BSOD.
column3: The screen and event logs can provide useful details for troubleshooting or escalation.
column4:
Why It Matters

The term is common in support tickets and interviews, but the useful action is to capture the error details rather than just naming the screen.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Cloze BSOD Memory Source::Messer-v170

column2: A system that loses date, time, or firmware settings on every boot often has a failed {{c1::motherboard battery}}.
column3: The same symptom pattern can appear after the system has been unplugged long enough for the weak battery to matter.
column4:
Exam Trap

Do not jump to OS repair when the symptom is firmware settings and time being lost before the OS matters.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Cloze CMOS Battery Memory Source::Messer-v170

column2: In hardware troubleshooting, rattling often suggests a loose component, while scraping may suggest a {{c1::mechanical drive issue}}.
column3: Noise clues are starting points. Confirm with inspection and diagnostics before replacing parts.
column4:
Why It Matters

Unusual noises are useful because they narrow the first inspection area, but they are not a final diagnosis by themselves.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Cloze Noises Memory Source::Messer-v170
